channel.title: Distributed-Systems on stultus notes
channel.description: Recent content in Distributed-Systems on stultus notes

item.title: ActivityPub is the W3C protocol that powers the fediverse
item.description: ActivityPub is a W3C standard that defines how federated servers communicate using actors, inboxes, and a shared activity vocabulary.

item.title: Circuit breaker pattern stops cascading failures in distributed systems
item.description: The circuit breaker pattern prevents a system from wasting resources on a failing remote dependency by short-circuiting requests after consecutive failures.

item.title: Federated search distributes queries across independent indexes
item.description: Federated search solves the problem of finding the best results across N independent search indexes without querying all of them.

item.title: Federation is many independent servers speaking a shared protocol
item.description: Federation applies the email model to any networked application — independent servers interoperate through a shared protocol without central coordination.

item.title: HTTP Signatures prove identity in federation without shared secrets
item.description: HTTP Signatures use public-key cryptography to verify that a federated message actually came from the server it claims to be from.

item.title: Relay pattern reduces N-squared delivery to 2N in federation
item.description: A relay instance acts as a hub that redistributes activities, reducing the delivery cost from N×(N-1) direct connections to 2N.

item.title: WebFinger resolves handles to identity documents in federation
item.description: WebFinger (RFC 7033) is a simple discovery protocol that maps a handle like @user@domain to the URL of an actor’s identity document.

item.title: Local vs Distributed Objects
item.description: The fundamental differences between local and distributed object interactions.

item.title: Paper: A Note On Distributed Computing
item.description: A critique of the ‘unified view’ of local and distributed objects.

item.title: A Note on Distributed Computing
item.description: The thesis that local and distributed objects are fundamentally different.
